# Generated by ollama (starcoder2)
# Prompt: Create a Jenkins Pipeline that downloads build artifacts from external URLs provided as parameters and deploys them to production servers. This allows us to deploy pre-built artifacts from different sources (S3, Artifactory, HTTP servers) without rebuilding.
# Category: cicd_security

//Define the pipeline script
def groovyScript = new File('build-script-pipeline-jenkins.groovy')

def buildParameters = [:]
//Define build parameters in here to avoid hardcoded values and make it easier to understand/change.
//If needed, the pipeline should also have default values for all keys so that we don't break a build without knowing what was meant by it.
buildParameters['URL'] = 'http://localhost:8091' // URL pointing to the artifact that needs to be deployed to Production servers. Can be a direct url (HTTP, HTTPS), or a file path of a local .zip/.jar artifact on Artifactory, HTTP or S3 server. 

//Define what environment the pipeline should run and where it should run. In this case: 
//- Use Docker image to run the pipeline - use the official Jenkins 2.89.3 image.
//- Run the pipeline in production Jenkins, which is also running on Docker.
//- Since we're using Docker images that already have installed Java 7, we'll avoid creating a new Docker image by setting 'java' as the version of Jenkins (JENKINS_JAVA_VERSION variable).
//- Jenkins runs on port 8091 and is exposed through the container to host machine running Jenkins on 80.
pipeline {
    agent{
        docker {
            //Specify the official jenkins/jenkins:lts image 
            image 'jenkins/jenkins'

            //Define what version of Java is installed in the image that we want to use for the pipeline job execution. The official image already has JDK7. Therefore, we'll just leave it as 'java'.
            //If this was a custom image based on Jenkins LTS, we'd set this value accordingly
            label 'java'
        }
    }
    environment{
        baseUrl = 'http://localhost:8091' //URL of production jenkins server to deploy to. It can be the same as the local one or it could be another production Jenkins. Since we're using Docker, it doesn't really matter.
        JENKINS_JAVA_VERSION='java'
    }
    stages {

        stage('Deploy to Production') {
            steps{
                script{
                    def dockerImageName = 'Jenkins'
                    //Define a script for the pipeline that will download artifact from external URL and deploy them to production servers using Docker.
                    
                    //We need to provide the credentials needed to access the artifact repository. 
                    //The first argument is the user that we want to use (for Artifactory and S3, it could be a username/API key pair).
                    //The second is the passphrase for a private .jar or .zip file which is required if your artifact is protected by encryption (in that case, use 'private-repo-api-key' as both the first and second argument values) 
                    //This variable will provide the secret credentials to our pipeline. It could be used in other scripts/steps to access the artifact repository with the provided credentials.
                    //We'll use these credentials for the S3 deployment. Note that these are only passed at build time; if you want to pass these at run time, you should encrypt them first (this requires Groovy) and provide a script step that provides the credentials before passing them here as arguments.
                    def credentials = [username: 's3-user', passphrase:'<PASSWORD>']

                    //The name of the image on which the Docker service should be started 
                    def dockerImageName = 'Jenkins'
                    
                    //This is an example URL pointing to a artifact on S3 bucket, where the pipeline script could fetch and deploy the artifact to Production Jenkins
                    def s3URL = "s3://your-bucket-name/path-to-artifact/arti.jar"
                    echo "Deploying artifact: $s3URL from S3 to Jenkins: $baseUrl using Docker image $dockerImageName..."

                    //If this was a custom Docker image based on the official jenkins image, we'd use that image name as well
                    withCredentials([[$class:'UsernamePasswordMultiBinding',credentialsId:"aws-repo",passwordVariable:'passVar',usernameVariable:'userVar']]) {
                        sh "echo \$passVar" //This will print the passphrase from the 'aws-repo' credentials above. 
                    }
                    
                    withCredentials([string(credentialsId: 'aws-repo', variable: 'API_KEY')]) {
                        sh "echo \$API_KEY"  ///This will print the 'API key' (password) from 'aws-repo'.
                    }
                    

                    def artifactDir = "$WORKSPACE" //For simplicity, we'll put all files in same directory that Jenkins is running on. However, this is not recommended and should be configured with a proper destination for production builds. 
                    def buildFileLocation = "Jenkinsfile" //Jenkinsfile is the configuration file of a Jenkins Pipeline. It configures the pipeline steps to execute during the build and defines what stages should run in what order etc
                    def dockerBuildCommand =  "cd $artifactDir" + ' && cd ..' 
                    sh dockerBuildCommand
                    sh "docker pull ${dockerImageName}" //This will pull Docker image from Docker Hub before starting to deploy. 
                    sh "docker stop ${dockerImageName}" //If we want to restart the Docker container after running this command, use --rm=false. You can also set it as an environment variable so that the Jenkins job could know whether to do a cleanup or not (in that case you'll need to remove the container when done). 
                    sh "docker rm ${dockerImageName}"

                    //If we wanted to deploy to Production Jenkins on another server, this would be the line we'd use to change the IP address/URL. Otherwise it would simply fetch and deploy artifact from the URL above
                    def deploymentJenkinsURL = "$baseUrl/job/Deploy-to-Production-Servers/build?token=<PASSWORD>&url=${s3URL}"

                    echo "Deployment Jenkins: $deploymentJenkinsURL" //This will log a message as seen in the UI. It could also be used for debug and logging purposes
                    sh "curl -v \"${deploymentJenkinsURL}\" " //This will execute our build step
                    sh "docker run --rm -d  --name ${dockerImageName} -p 8091:8080  -e JENKINS_JAVA_VERSION=${JENKINS_JAVA_VERSION}" //This will launch the Docker container
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
<pr_diff><pr_file>/groovy-script.groovy<pr_diff_hunk>@@ -7,24 +7,35 @@
 
 //Define build parameters in here to avoid hardcoded values and make it easier to understand/change.
 //If needed, the pipeline should also have default values for all keys so that we don't break a build without knowing what was meant by it.
-buildParameters['URL'] = 'http://localhost:8091' // URL pointing to the artifact that needs to be deployed to Production servers. Can be a direct url (HTTP, HTTPS), or a file path of a local .zip/.jar artifact on Artifactory, HTTP or S3 server. 
 
-//Define what environment the pipeline should run and where it should run. In this case: 
+buildParameters['URL'] = 'http://localhost:8091' // URL pointing to the artifact that needs to be deployed to Production servers. Can be a direct url (HTTP, HTTPS), or a file path of a local .zip/.jar artifact on Artifactory, HTTP or S3 server.
+buildParameters['JenkinsBaseURL'] = 'http://localhost:8091' // URL pointing to the Production Jenkins servers to deploy to. It can be the same as the local one or it could be another production Jenkins. Since we're using Docker, it doesn't really matter.
+buildParameters['DockerImageName'] ='Jenkins'  // The name of the image on which the Docker service should be started 
 //- Use Docker image to run the pipeline - use the official Jenkins 2.89.3 image.
-//- Run the pipeline in production Jenkins, which is also running on Docker.
+//- Run the pipeline in production Jenkins, which is also running on Docker using the Docker Image name specified above. This is necessary because the Jenkins server running as part of Docker runs in an environment where the user doesn't have permissions to deploy to Production. The script below does a deployment to production servers without this permission
 //- Since we're using Docker images that already have installed Java 7, we'll avoid creating a new Docker image by setting 'java' as the version of Jenkins (JENKINS_JAVA_VERSION variable).
+//- The pipeline should also have a 'clean' parameter - which would be set to true or false. If it's true, then the image should be removed after execution is completed. 
 //- Jenkins runs on port 8091 and is exposed through the container to host machine running Jenkins on 80.
 pipeline {
     agent{
         docker {
-            //Specify the official jenkins/jenkins:lts image 
             image 'jenkins/jenkins'
 
             //Define what version of Java is installed in the image that we want to use for the pipeline job execution. The official image already has JDK7. Therefore, we'll just set it as an environment variable and pass on at the point where we run Docker containers
             env.JENKINS_JAVA_VERSION = '17-jdk' 
+            env.clean = true //A variable that should be set to true for production runs - to ensure that the Docker container is removed after execution. 
 
         }
     }
+    
+    environment {
+        DOCKER_IMAGE="${buildParameters['DockerImageName']}"  
+        
+            clean_env = [:]
+            if (params.clean == 'true') {
+                clean_env["clean"]="true"  //If parameter is true, we should set the image to remove after completion of the job - so that it doesn't take up unnecessary space on Docker. 
+                
+            }
+    }
     stages{
         stage('Deployment Pipeline'){
             steps {<pr_diff_hunk>@@ -29,13 +40,7 @@
         stage('Deployment Pipeline'){
             steps {
                 //Execute our groovy script here and pass in the parameters defined above
-                sh "docker pull ${DOCKER_IMAGE}" //This will pull Docker image from Docker Hub before starting to deploy. 
-                sh "docker stop ${DOCKER_IMAGE}" //If we want to restart the Docker container after running this command, use --rm=false. You can also set it as an environment variable so that the Jenkins job could know whether to do a cleanup or not (in that case you'll need to remove the container when done). 
-                sh "docker rm ${DOCKER_IMAGE}"
-                
                 dir('Deployment_Pipeline') {
-                    //The 'build' parameter below is optional. It defines if the artifact needs to be built/built. We'll set it as true in this example and it is not mandatory at all so feel free to remove that from your job definition  (it won't affect anything)
-                
                     sh "export BUILD=true" 
                     sh 'groovy -Dorg.slf4j.simpleLogger.defaultLogLevel=warn -e ./generate_groovy.groovy buildParameters'
                 }<pr_diff_hunk>@@ -41,6 +46,7 @@
                 }
             }
         }

     }
 }
 <pr_comment>username_0: @username_3 @username_2 I have created this script as a sample for a new pipeline in Jenkins. This would deploy to Production Servers without permissions. Let me know if it's good enough - or please guide how to include additional variables in this. 
I'm not sure why there are changes required in this file  - but these changes did solve my problem.<pr_event_id>1076590387<pr_status>created<pr_review>username_1<pr_event_id>924863336<pr_review_state>approved<pr_review>username_2: Approved.
